questions.question: What are the seven components of the capstone voice-assistant pipeline?
options: Audio capture (16 kHz mono, 20ms chunks) → VAD → streaming STT → LLM with tool calling → streaming TTS → playback → interruption handler
questions.explanation: Everything from lessons 01-11 stitched together. The latency target: first TTS audio byte within 800ms of the user finishing, on a laptop CPU.

questions.question: Why start TTS after ~20 LLM tokens instead of waiting for the full response?
options: Pipelining: streaming the LLM's first sentence into TTS while the rest generates overlaps the two slowest stages — waiting for the complete response would blow the latency budget
The first 20 tokens are the most accurate
questions.explanation: The same streaming-everywhere principle from lesson 11: no stage may batch-and-wait. Kokoro-82M (fastest open) or Cartesia Sonic (commercial) handle the synthesis side.

questions.question: What are the three failure modes you will hit, and their fixes?
options: GPU OOM, disk full, network down

First-word clip (VAD starts late — lower the start threshold to 0.3), mid-response interrupt confusion (wire VAD directly to cancel-LLM), and silence hallucination (Whisper emits "Thanks for watching" on silence — always VAD-gate)
Wrong language, wrong voice, wrong volume
questions.explanation: Whisper's silence hallucination comes from its YouTube-heavy training data — subtitle-like phantom text on empty audio is a known signature, and VAD-gating is the standard defense.

questions.question: What do the recommended VAD settings (threshold 0.5, min speech 250ms, hang-over 500ms) accomplish?
options: Min-speech filters out coughs and clicks from triggering the pipeline; the 500ms silence hang-over avoids cutting the user off at natural mid-sentence pauses; the threshold balances sensitivity vs false triggers
questions.explanation: These three knobs encode turn-taking behavior. Too aggressive a hang-over makes the assistant interrupt the user; too long makes it feel sluggish to respond.

questions.question: How do the 2026 production stacks differ?
options: LiveKit + Deepgram + GPT-4o + Cartesia is the commercial default (350-500ms); Pipecat + Whisper-streaming + Kokoro is DIY-friendly (500-800ms); Moshi is single-model full-duplex (200-300ms); Whisper.cpp + llama.cpp + Kokoro-ONNX runs fully offline for privacy/edge
Only one stack works in production
questions.explanation: The axis is latency vs control vs privacy. Moshi's full-duplex single-model design is architecturally different — covered in lesson 15.
